source_metadata.title_raw: Prentice Fish - A-82-022 - March 16, 1982 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
A-82-029

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 327-5562        322-3660           322-5901          392-6441          322-6444

March 16, 1982


Prentice Fish
Deputy County Counsel
County Administration Center
2555 Mendocino Avenue
Santa Rosa, CA  95401

Dear Mr. Fish:

     Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this
office on behalf of Mr. Hack Boyett, a member of the Sonoma
County Local Agency Formation Commission. Based on our
conversation, your letter of February 9, 1982, and the other
correspondence attached to your letter, my understanding of
the situation is as follows.

     The Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission
(LAFCO) is organized pursuant to Government Code Sections
54773, et seq.  It approves or denies requests for the
annexation of property to cities. The Sonoma County LAFCO
has a written policy requiring the applicant-city to prezone
the area proposed for annexation before it will act on the
proposal. If the annexation is approved, further city permits
will have to be acquired before actual development can proceed.

     In our phone conversation in October and your letter of
September 29, 1981, the Frates Ranch Annexation proposal was
the subject of discussion. This annexation proposal, which
was subsequently withdrawn before LAFCO had voted on it,
involved approximately 700 acres. It was proposed that
approximately 200 acres would be used for a business park,
105 acres for approximately 600 residential units, and 232
acres for a golf course. Approximately 60 acres of Frates
Ranch is owned by Pacific Gas and Electric Company which
uses it presently as a substation.

_____________________
1/  All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Prentice Fish
March 16, 1982
Page Two


Hack Boyett, the public member of Sonoma County LAFCO,
is the Sonoma County Regional Manager for the Pacific Gas
and Electric Company. He also owns stock valued between
$10,000 and $100,000 in the Pacific Gas and Electric Company.

You concluded in your letter of September 29, 1981, and
I concurred, that Mr. Boyett would be required to disqualify
himself and not participate in any way in LAFCO's decision
on the Frates Ranch annexation proposal since the foreseeable
financial effect on PG&E's annual gross revenues far exceeded
$100,000. This conclusion was based in part on Commission
Regulation 18702(b)(A)(1) which reads in relevant part:

(1) Whether, in the case of a business entity in
which the public official holds a direct or indirect
investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more or
in the case of a business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, employee,
trustee or holds any position of management, the effect
of the decision will be to increase or decrease:

(A) The annualized gross revenues by the
lesser of:

1. One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);
or

2. One percent if it is one thousand
dollars $1,000) or more.

Even though the Frates Ranch annexation proposal is no
longer before LAFCO for decision, you requested that we
clarify the guidelines contained in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(1) for determining a material financial effect.
Since you have not described a specific decision pending
before LAFCO for our consideration, the following advice is
not provided pursuant to Section 83114(b). However, this
discussion should provide you with guidance as LAFCO considers
other annexation proposals which may be expected to increase
PG&E's annual gross revenues.

Two Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 sets forth guidelines
for determining whether a foreseeable financial effect is
also material. Subsection (a) of the regulation provides
general guidelines while subsection (b) includes specific
monetary tests for materiality. Generally speaking, the
percentage tests in subsection (b) should be followed when-
ever possible. Where the financial impact of a decision is

Prentice Fish
March 16, 1982
Page Three


difficult or impossible to calculate, the general test in
subsection (a) is the appropriate measure of materiality.
If the effect is material under either the general test of
significance in subsection (a) or the percentage tests in
subsection (b), the element of materiality is generally
considered to be present.

In borderline cases, all relevant facts would be considered
in making the determination of materiality. Each situation
has to be considered separately, and there could conceivably
be factors which would indicate in a borderline case that
the effect was not material within the meaning of Section
87103. Normally, however, if the $100,000 threshold of
Section 18702(b)(1)(A)(i) is met, the effect is considered
material, and other factors will not change that conclusion.

Some factors have not been considered in the determination
of materiality. The size of the total annual gross revenues
of a business entity has not ever been used to find an effect
immaterial when that effect exceeds $100,000. The Act does
not exclude effects on very large business entities, and the
$100,000 threshold was intended to provide a benchmark for
determining when an effect on any business entity including
a very large business entity will be considered material.

Neither does the Act exempt public utilities or other
heavily regulated business entities from its coverage.
While the fact that an entity is subject to certain government
regulations out of its control may be relevant to a determina-
tion of materiality, there is no automatic exemption from
the conflict of interest provisions for effects on public
utilities. See Opinion requested by Vincent F. Biondo, Jr.,
1 FPPC 54 (1975).

Lastly, while we are certainly sympathetic to the goal
of encouraging public service, the Act itself represents a
public policy determination that all "public officials,
whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in
an impartial manner free from bias caused by their own
financial interests..." Section 81001(b). The conflict of
interest provisions of the Act define when an official has a
financial interest in a decision. The Commission Regulations
and other interpretations of the Act attempt to further the
public policy embodied in the Act. On occasion, the application
of the Act's provisions may seem unfair, but overall the
approach works well in the majority of cases.

Prentice Fish
March 16, 1982
Page Four


     To the Prates Ranch matter, it was clear that the effect
on PG&E's annual gross revenues would be far in excess of
$100,000.  Your figures for computing the effect did not
take into account revenues to be generated by the business
park, hotel or golf course.2/  In addition, the rate estimates
were intentionally conservative, and effects on PG&E's real
property interest in the annexation area were not included.
The cumulative total effect on PG&E from the Prates Ranch
annexation may well have exceeded $1 million.  Thus that
situation was not a borderline case where other factors
could have tipped the balance.

     If other situations arise which appear to be borderline
in determining the materiality of an effect, we will be
happy to advise you on the determination.  I have enclosed a
recent staff letter to Scott R. Keene, City Attorney for
Petaluma, which discussed many of the same issues raised by
your request.

     If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to
contact me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:km
Enclosure




_____________________
2/  This was understandable since the computations for
the residential units alone indicated an effect of $236,000.

JAMES P. BOTZ
COUNTY COUNSEL

OFFICE OF THE

COUNTY COUNSEL
COUNTY OF SONOMA

COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
2555 MENDOCINO AVENUE
SANTA ROSA, CALIFORNIA 95401
TELEPHONE (707) 527-2421

ASSISTANT
JOHN C GAFFNEY
CHIEF DEPUTY
LYNDA MILLSPAUGH
DEPUTIES
RICHARD W ERGO
PRENTICE A FISH
CAROLINE A KERL
MARK J FREED
SUZAN D HATFIELD

February 9, 1982

Ms.Diane Fishburn
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Fishburn:

This office represents the Sonoma County Local Agency
Formation Commission. One of the Commission's members--Mr. Hack Boyett--
has asked me to request an advice letter from you regarding his
obligations under the Political Reform Act. I have talked with
you by telephone about Mr. Boyett's situation back in October
of 1981. I would appreciate it if you would forward this letter,
together with the enclosures, to the appropriate personnel for
review and the issuance of an advice letter.

I am enclosing several documents which may be of
assistance to you. First, I am enclosing my four-page letter
of September 29, 1981, to Mr. Boyett. I might note that the
estimated monthly utility bill set forth on page three of my
letter is obviously low at the present time given the recent
increases in utility company rates.

Second, I am enclosing a two-page legal memorandum
from Mr. J. I. Kelly, a Pacific Gas and Electric Company lawyer.

Third, I am enclosing a two-page letter dated October 16,
1981, to me from Mr. Boyett.

Fourth, I am enclosing a four-page letter to me from
Mr. Richard Maxwell, an attorney and friend of Mr. Boyett.

Lastly, I am enclosing a two-page letter dated January 20,
1982, to me from Mr. Boyett.

The Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission is
a governmental commission established under Government Code
section 54773 and following. One of the Commission's duties is
the prevention of urban sprawl. The Commission approves or denies
requests for the annexation of property (usually undeveloped) to
cities. Annexation requests are typically filed with the Commission

Ms. Diane Fishburn
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices
Commission

February 9, 1932
Page 2

when development of the property is proposed and city sewer and
water service is necessary. The Sonoma County Local Agency
Formation Commission has established a written policy requiring
the affected city to prezone the area proposed for annexation
before LAFCO will act on the annexation proposal. The commissioners
have required prezoning so that they will know, in general terms,
the expected type of development--either residential, commercial,
industrial, or some mix thereof--as well as the expected density.
If the Commission approves an annexation, actual development will
require further discretionary city permits.

One such annexation request--the Frates Ranch
Annexation--was the subject of my letter of September 29, 1981, to
Mr. Boyett. Although that annexation proposal was subsequently
withdrawn and LAFCO never had to approve or disapprove it, the
facts surrounding that annexation proposal generally illustrates
Mr. Boyett's concerns. Basically, in Mr. Boyett's case, the issue
boils down to whether or not the $100,000 or more gross revenue
rule set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702 (b) (1) (A) bars
him from participating as a commissioner from matters coming
before the Commission where the expected gross revenues from
the proposed development will exceed $100,000. If you conclude
that Mr. Boyett is not barred by the Political Reform Act from
participating in a LAFCO decision where the decision may increase
the annual gross revenues of Pacific Gas and Electric Company
by $100,000 or more, the question would then arise as to what is
the proper threshold level. $200,000? $300,000? $1,000,000?

As the Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission
considers various development proposals which, because of size,
may be expected to increase the annual gross revenues of Pacific
Gas and Electric Company in varying amounts, I would appreciate
a general consideration of the $1000,000 annual gross revenue
rule. If, however, you need more specific facts, please do not
hesitate to give either myself or Mr. Boyett a call. Mr. Boyett
is the Sonoma County Regional Manager for the Pacific Gas and
Electric Company and he may be reached at (707) 544-2220.

Yours truly,

PRENTICE FISH
Deputy County Counsel

cc: Hack Boyett
Richard Maxwell
James Moore

JAMES P. BOTZ
COUNTY COUNSEL

OFFICE OF THE

COUNTY COUNSEL

ASSISTANT
JOHN C GAFFNEY

COUNTY OF SONOMA
COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
2555 MENDOCINO AVENUE
SANTA ROSA, CALIFORNIA 95401
TELEPHONE (707) 527-2421

CHIEF DEPUTY
LYNDA MILLSPAUGH

DEPUTIES
RICHARD W. ERGO
PRENTICE A. FISH
CAROLINE A. KERL
MARY J. FREED
SUZAN D HATFIELD

September 29, 1981

Hack Boyett, Commissioner
Sonoma County Local Agency
Formation Commission
c/o Pacific Gas and Electric Company
3965 Occidental Road
Santa Rosa, California 9540)

Re: Frates Ranch Annexation--Potential
Conflict of Interest

Dear Mr. Boyett:

On September 25, 1981, you asked whether or not you
might have a potential conflict of interest in voting on the
proposed Frates Ranch annexation to the City of Petaluma.
That annexation will come before the Sonoma County Local
Formation Commission on October 1, 1981. The facts applicable
to your situation, together with my opinion based on those
facts, follows. As always, my opinion is strictly limited to
the factual situation set forth below. If the facts change
materially, my opinion may well change. If the facts set out
below are not complete or are in error, please let me know
immediately. I appreciate the fact that you have contacted me
prior to the LAFCO meeting. This has given me an opportunity
to research the matter and thus avoid a continuance of the
Frates Ranch matter.

FACTS

Petaluma proposes to annex the Frates Ranch and
other acreage to Petaluma. The Sonoma County Local Agency
Formation Commission will hear the annexation proposal on
October 1, 1981. Commissioner Hack Boyett is the public
member of the Commission. He is now and has been for some
time the manager of Pacific Gas and Electric Company in charge
of the Sonoma County area.

Petaluma proposes to annex approximately 700 acres.
Included in that annexation proposal is approximately 60 acres
of property owned by Pacific Gas and Electric Company. That
60 acres is presently used as a substation by the utility
company.

Hack Boyett
Page Two

September 29, 1981

If annexed, approximately 200 acres of the Frates
Ranch will be used as a business park including a hotel and
commercial development. An additional 105 acres is proposed
for mixed residential--single-family residential and multiple
units--consisting of approximately 600 residential units.
Approximately 232 acres would be used as a golf course.

Commissioner Boyett has previously disclosed that he
owns stock valued between $10,000 and $100,000 in the Pacific
Gas and Electric Company. Neither Commissioner Boyett nor any
members of his family owns real property within the area proposed for annexation or in the nearby area.

ISSUE

Must Commissioner Boyett disqualify himself from
voting upon the Frates Ranch annexation proposal?

ANSWER

If the entire Frates Ranch development would in-
crease the annual gross revenues (not not income) of Pacific
Gas and Electric Company by more than $100,000, the answer is
yes. Since it is virtually certain that the annual gross
revenues would be increased by more than $100,000, Commis-
sioner Boyett must disqualify himself.

DISCUSSION

The basic statutory rule governing potential con-
flicts of interest is set forth in Government Code §87100 as
follows:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to in-
fluence a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest."

What constitutes a "financial interest" is defined
by Government Code §87103. That section provides:

"An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of §87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material

Hack Boyett
Page Three

September 29, 1981

financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public of-
ficial has a direct or indirect investment worth
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).....

(d) Any business entity in which the public of-
ficial is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management."
(Emphasis added.)

Since Commissioner Boyett has an investment worth
more than $1,000 in P G & E and since he holds a position of
management in that company, Commissioner Boyett would have a
financial interest in LAFCO's decision within the meaning of
Government Code §87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that
LAFCO's decision will have a "material financial effect" on
P G & E.

The Fair Political Practices Commission has promul-
gated a regulation describing what constitutes a "material
financial effect". Basically, the regulation sets forth
three alternative tests. Those tests are (1) the annual gross
revenue test and (2) the annual net income test and (3) the
current asset test. 2 Cal.Admin.Code §18702(b).

Under the Commission's regulations, a governmental
decision that will increase or decrease the annual gross
revenues by $100,000 or more is "significant" and will have a
"material financial effect". Turning now to the facts of this
case, given today's utility bills one can safely assume that
an average monthly utility bill for the 600 units proposed for
the Fratres Ranch development will be at least $40 per month.
The annual utility bill per unit will thus be at least $480.
P G & E's total annual revenues from the 600 proposed units
would be $288,000. This exceeds the $100,000 gross revenue
test promulgated by the Fair Political Practices Commission.
Therefore, Commissioner Boyett must disqualify himself and not
participate in any way in LAFCO's decision on that annexation
proposal.

Given the facts outlined above, there is no need to
discuss whether or not Commissioner Boyett also meets the
capital asset test that would be applied to the 60-acre
Pacific Gas and Electric Company property. Likewise, there is

Hack Boyett                                    September 29, 1981
Page Four


no need to determine whether or not the annual net income test
is met.

     I have reviewed the facts of this case and the ap-
plicable law with the staff at the Fair Practices
Commission in Sacramento. The staff concurs with this opin-
ion. Should you have any further questions, please give me a
call. I am enclosing for your perusal a copy of Government
Code §§87100, 87103 and 2 Cal.Admin.Code §18702.

                                        Yours truly,


                                        PRENTICE FISH
                                        Deputy County Counsel

PF:vr

Enclosures

cc: James Moore, Executive Officer,
    LAFCO

§ 87100. Public officials; state and local; financial interest

No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

(Added by Initiative Measure approved by the electors June 4, 1974, eff. Jan. 7, 1975.)


§ 87103. Financial interest; material effect; indirect investment or interest

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, upon:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, excepting two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employer, or holds any position of management.

For purpose of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, * * * or by * * * a business entity * * * or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children * * * own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

* * *

(Amended by Stats.1979, c. 680, p. —, § 2; Stats.1980, c. 183, p. —, § 2.)

TITLE 2 FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION § 18702
(a) (5) (B) No. 27—7-7-79

§ 18702. Material Financial Effect (87103).
(a) The financial effect of a governmental decision on a financial interest of
a public official is material if the decision will have a significant effect on the
business entity, real property or source of income in question.

(b) In determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the effects of
a governmental decision will be significant within the meaning of the general
standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration should be given to the following factors:

(1) Whether, in the case of a business entity in which the public official holds
a direct or indirect investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more or in
the case of a business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, employee, trustee or holds any position of management, the effect of
the decision will be to increase or decrease:

(A) The annualized gross revenues by the lesser of
1. One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000), or
2. One percent if it is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more, or

(B) Annual net income by the lesser of:
1. Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000), or
2. One half of one percent if it is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more, or

(C) Current assets or liabilities by the lesser of
1. One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000), or
2. One half of one percent if it is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
Current assets are deemed to be decreased by the amount of any expenses
incurred as a result of a governmental decision

(2) Whether, in the case of a direct or indirect interest in real property of
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more held by a public official, the effect of the
decision will be to increase or decrease:

(A) The income producing potential of the property by the lesser of
1. One thousand dollars ($1,000) per month, or
2. Five percent per month if it is fifty dollars ($50) or more per month, or

(B) The fair market value of the property by the lesser of
1. Ten thousand dollars ($10,000), or
2. One half of one percent if it is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more

(3) Whether, in the case of a source of income, as defined in Government
Code Section 87103(c), of two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more received by
or promised to a public official within 12 months prior to the time the decision
is made:

(A) The effect of the decision will be to directly increase or decrease the
amount of income (other than rents) to be received by the official by one
hundred dollars ($100) or more, or

(B) There is a nexus between the governmental decision and the purpose
for which the official receives income, or

(C) In the case of a source of income which is a business entity, the business
entity will be affected in a manner described in subsection (b) (1) above, or

(D) If the source of income is not a business entity, the decision will have
a significant effect on the source.

(c) Subsections (a) and (b) of this section notwithstanding, the making or
participation in the making of a governmental decision by a contract consultant
or by a person retained to provide information, advice, recommendation or
counsel has no material financial effect on a business entity or source of income
in which the consultant or person retained is an officer, employee, sole proprietor or partner, if the only financial effects of the decision are the modification, perpetuation or renewal of the contractual or retainer agreement and/or
the opportunity to bid competitively on a project or contract

NOTE Authority cited: Section 83112, Government Code. Reference: Section 87103,
Government Code.

HISTORY:
1. New section filed 1-22-76, effective thirtieth day thereafter (Register 76, No. 4).
2. Amendment filed 3-19-78 as an emergency, effective upon filing Certificate of
Compliance included (Register 78, No. 12).
3. Amendment filed 12-20-78, effective thirtieth day thereafter (Register 78, No. 51).
4. Amendment of subsection (a) and (b) filed 7-5-79, effective thirtieth day thereafter
(Register 79, No. 27).

• 67-8210 [REV 8/78]

PG andE
FOR INTRA-COMPANY USES

From Division or
Department

LAW

FILE NO.

RE LETTER OF
SUBJECT

Frates Ranch Annexation-Potential Conflict of Interest

To Division or
Department

October 15, 1981

MR. HACK BOYETT:

Pursuant to your request, I have reviewed the
September 29, 1981 letter to you from Deputy County Counsel,
Prentice Fish in which he concludes that you must disqualify
yourself from voting on the Frates Ranch development.

Mr. Fish correctly states that you have a financial
interest in a decision concerning the Frates Ranch development
because you have a direct investment worth more than $1,000
in PGandE and because you are an employee and manager of PGandE.
Consequently, if it is reasonably foreseeable that your decision
will have a "material financial effect" on PGandE, you must
disqualify yourself. Mr. Fish concludes that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the LAFCO decision will have a material
financial effect because your decision may increase the annual
gross revenues of PGandE by $100,000 or more.

However, the regulation cited by Mr. Fish provides
that in determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the effects of governmental decision will be significant, con-
sideration should be given to several factors, including whether
the effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease the
annualized gross revenues of PGandE by $100,000. In other
words, the regulation does not provide that every case involving
$100,000 necessarily has a material financial effect, but sets
forth that this factor should be considered in determining the
question.

Consequently, while the increase in annualized gross
revenues to PGandE of over $100,000 must be considered, it is
not dispositive. Other factors, including the fact that PGandE
had 1980 total operating revenues of $5,259,000,000, should
also be considered. That is, even assuming that the gross
annualized revenues of the Company will increase by $288,000
(see Fish Opn., p. 3), this figure represents only 5/1000 of
1% of the Company's total operating revenues. Put another way,
this figure only represents 5 1/2 cents for each $1,000 in
operating revenue.

Mr. Hack Boyett                    -2-                         October 15, 1981


     I assume that Mr. Fish was not provided with the
Company's total operating revenue when he arrived at his con-
clusion. Since it is my opinion that these additional facts
may result in a different opinion, I recommend that you provide
a copy of this memorandum to Mr. Fish, along with a request for
another written opinion.


                                   J. I. KELLY

JIK(2286)·md

October 16, 1981

Mr. Prentice Fish
Deputy County Counsel
County of Sonoma
2555 Mendocino Avenue
Santa Rosa, California 95401

Dear Mr. Fish:

Thank you for your letter of September 29, 1981, in which you informed me that since it is virtually certain that the annual gross revenues of PGandE would be increased by more than $100,000 by the development of the Frates Ranch Plan, I must disqualify myself from voting on this matter. You also stated that if the facts set out in your letter are not complete, I should let you know.

Since receipt of the letter, it has come to my attention that there are material facts which could well result in an alteration of your conclusion.

As the attached memorandum from Joseph I. Kelly of Pacific Gas and Electric Company Law Department points out, Title 2, California Administrative Code, section 18702 provides that one of the factors to be considered in determining whether the financial effect of the governmental decision on a financial interest of a public official is material is whether the effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease the annualized gross revenues of a business entity by $100,000. Significantly, this regulation does not provide that every case involving a $100,000 increase or decrease will per se have a material financial effect, but sets forth that this is one factor to be considered. Consequently, while the factors listed in section 18702 must be considered, they are not necessarily dispositive. Other factors, including the fact that PGandE had 1980 total operating revenues of $5,259,000,000, should also be considered. 1/

In other words, assuming that the development is totally constructed and results in annual gross revenue increase of $288,000 (see p. 3 of your opinion), this figure represents approximately 5/1000 of 1% of PGandE's 1980 total operating revenues. Put another way, the LAFCO decision may only increase or decrease the gross revenues of the Company by 5 1/2 cents per $1,000 of gross revenue.

Consequently, in light of total operating revenues compared with any expected increase realized by the development, it does not appear that any decision I might make could be considered to have a material financial effect on PGandE.

___________________________________________________________________________

1. See the enclosed 1980 Annual Report of PGandE, Financial Highlights, p. 1.

Mr. Prentice Fish                    -2-                    October 16, 1981


I would appreciate your considering these additional facts and issuing
another opinion in view of the information contained in this letter.
I regret that the total operating figures were not made available to
you when I first asked your advice, but did not realize that they were
pertinent at the time of my initial inquiry.

Your prompt attention to this matter would be appreciated.

                                                    Sincerely,

                                                    [signature]
                                                    H. E. BOYETT

LAW OFFICES OF

CHAMBER PLAZA - 635 FIRST STREET
SANTA ROSA, CALIFORNIA 95404

(707) 546-1022

BRYCE SWARTFAEGER
(1895-1969)

CABLE ADDRESS:
MAXALL, SANTA ROSA,
CALIFORNIA

January 13, 1982

Mr. Prentice Fish
Deputy County Counsel
County Administration Center
575 Administration Drive, Room 116A
Santa Rosa, CA  95401

RECEIVED
JAN 22 1982

County Counsel
COUNTY OF SONOMA

Re:  H. E. Boyett; Conflict of Interest;
     Local Agency Formation Commission

Dear Prentice:

     This letter is written to you on behalf of my long time
friend, Mr. H. E. Boyett, who serves as manager for Pacific Gas
& Electric Company in the Sonoma County area and also is an appointed
member on the Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission.

     It is my understanding that you will shortly be submitting
a request for an advisory opinion to the Executive Director of the
California Fair Political Practice Commission concerning the
question of a possible conflict of interest on the part of Mr.
Boyett when acting upon annexation projects such as the Petaluma
annexation and the Frates residential/industrial project.  It is my
further understanding that the chief concern in connection with
such matters is the possibility that the Frates project, or similar
projects, if completed, would generate an annual gross revenue from
the sale of natural gas and electricity to Pacific Gas & Electric
Company in excess of the $100,000.00 amount suggested by the regulations of the Fair Political Practice Commission.

     As an attorney who has served as political advisor for
various local public agencies over the past twenty-five years I
welcomed the enactment of the Political Reform Act of 1974 as a means
of giving some definition to the continuing problem of conflict
of interest.  However, I am equally concerned with interpretations
of that act which place an unfair burden on the conscientious efforts
of people like Mr. Boyett who donate their time and effort to
public services.

     Accordingly I would request that consideration be given
by you or the staff of the Fair Political Practice Commission to
the following:

THE FINANCIAL INTEREST MUST BE SIGNIFICANT.

     A public official is not to act upon any matter in which
he knows he had a "financial interest."  (Gov. Code §87100)  An

Mr. Prentice Fish
Page Two
January 13, 1982

official has a financial interest when it is "reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect" on
either his ownership interest or management responsibilities in
a "business entity." (See Gov. Code 7103) The financial effect
of the governmental decision or financial interest of a public
official is material only if the decision will have a "significant
effect" on the business entity, and in this regard some "consideration" should be given to a circumstance where the annual gross
revenue of the business entity would be increased by $100,000 or
more. (Regs. 18702)

The regulation cited above indicates that any increase
in gross annual revenues in excess of $100,000 is only a "consideration" to be utilized in determining whether the project would
have a "significant effect" on the financial interest of the
public officer. It is my understanding that the estimated annual
revenue of the Petaluma Annexation (Frates Project) is in excess
of $100,000 and perhaps as high as $288,000 per year. When you
consider that this figure constitutes only 1/200th of one percent
of the gross annual revenue of PG&E for 1980 totalling five and
one-quarter billion dollars ($5,250,000,000), and when this is
accompanied by the fact that Mr. Boyett is only a local manager for
PG&E it seems difficult to believe that any reasonably minded person
would conclude that such would have a material effect upon a
"financial interest" of Mr. Boyett.

CONSIDERATION SHOULD BE GIVEN TO THE FACT THAT PG&E IS A QUASI-
PUBLIC ENTITY AND NOT STRICTLY A PRIVATE BUSINESS ENTITY.

Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act and the regulations
promulgated to assist in the interpretation of that Act were drafted
with private profit making business entities in mind. (See Gov.
Code §82005) In applying these rules and regulations it should be
considered that a quasi-public entity such as Pacific Gas & Electric
Company cannot operate in the same manner as other large private
businesses such as Exxon, Sears, etc. These large profit making
companies when considering a project which is subject to the

Mr. Prentice Fish
Page Three
January 13, 1982

discretionary review of a local public agency, will presumably
proceed with the project if such project can be carried out at a
profit. PG&E is a quasi-public entity providing gas and electric
service to all development projects which services are just as
essential to the project as public sewer and water. PG&E, as a
highly regulated governmental monopoly, has no discretion as to
whether or not to provide these services to the project. Whether
such services will be provided to a specific project at a profit
depends not upon the project itself but on the large picture of
the company's operation as a whole as determined by the State Public
Utilities Commission. This is not the type of beneficial
"financial interest" which the framers of the Political Reform Act
had in mind when drafting its provisions and promulgating its
regulations. This is not to say that all public utilities are
not within the purview of the Political Reform Act, as there may
well be cases where the public utility itself is applying for a
discretionary development permit which would be a benefit strictly
to the public utility company itself. However where the public
utility is simply performing the function required of it by the
State of California in supplying gas and electric power to develop-
ment projects within the State, it would not seem to be receiving
the beneficial "financial" effect anticipated by Chapter 7 of the
Political Reform Act.

ANY MATERIAL FINANCIAL EFFECT MUST BE "REASONABLY FORESEEABLE";
LAFCO HAS NO AUTHORITY TO APPROVE OR REJECT A DEVELOPMENT.

A public officer has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act only if it is
"reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect" upon him. (Gov. Code §87100)

The Political Reform Act, and the regulations thereunder,
were drafted with the thought in mind that the public officer is
acting upon a discretionary permit which, if granted, would allow
the applicant to proceed with a development which is of some
benefit to the applicant. Such is not the case with a Local Agency

Mr. Prentice Fish
Page Four
January 13, 1982

Formation Commission which is specifically prohibited by statute
from approving or disapproving or in any manner regulating any
development project. (Gov. Code §54790(a)(3)) The only issue
before LAFCO is as to whether or not the specific property
should or should not be annexed to a City. It is only by the sub-
sequent action of the City, acting under its Subdivision and
Zoning Ordinances, that it is determined whether some, if any,
type of development will or will not be approved within the area
annexed. This makes it extremely difficult for a member of LAFCO
when called upon to act upon an annexation, to "reasonly foresee"
that he may or may not have a material financial interest in
some project which the City may see fit to approve within the
annexed area.

INTERPRETATIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT.

The problem of Mr. Boyett, as a member of LAFCO, in
determining whether or not he is faced with a conflict of interest
under the Political Reform Act, is not one that can be considered
in isolation. There are undoubtedly large numbers of other highly
qualified employees of the major utility companies serving Calif-
ornia, i.e. PG&E, So. Cal. Edison, Mut. Water Dist. Sacto. M.U.D.,
Pac. Tel. & Tel. et al, who donate the services to local government.
Certainly it is sound public policy to encourage such service.
Interpretations of the Political Reform Act should not be so strained
as to seriously interfere with the tremendous public benefits
obtained from those who donate their time and energy to public service.

It is sincerely hoped that your office as well as the
staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission will see fit to
recognize the remoteness of the financial interest of Mr.Boyett to
the annexation projects, which come before him in his capacity as
a member of LAFCO.

Respectfully submitted,

MAXWELL, ALLEN & COOPER
By___________________________
Richard B. Maxwell
RBM:er
cc: Mr. H. E. Boyett

sections.analysis:
The basic statutory ru 
governing potential con-
ficts of interest is set forth in Government Code 587100 as 
follows: 
"No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, partici 
te in making or in 
any way attempt to use his official position to in-
fluence a governmental decision in which he knows or 
has reason to know he 
s a financial intere t." 
What constitutes a "financial interest" is defined 
by Government Code §87l03. 
That section 
ovides: 
"An off c 
1 has a financi 
in erest in a d cis on 
within the meaning of S87 
0

is reason 
forese 
that the decisi 
will 
ave 
i 


Hack Boyett 
Page Three 
Sept 
r 29, 1981 
t, distinguishab e from its effect 
----------~-~-~---

(a) 
business entity in 
ich the public of-
ficial has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more than one thousand do I rs (S1,OOO) •.•. 
(d) Any business entity in 
ficial is a director, officer, 
employee, or holds any position 
(Emphasis added.) 
which the public of-
rtner, trustee, 
of management." 
Since Commissioner Boyett has an investment worth 
more than $1,000 in P G & E and since he holds a position of 
management in that company, Commissioner Boyett would have a 
financial interest in LAFCO's decision within the meaning of 
Government Code §87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
LAFCO's decision will have a "material financial effect" on 
P G & E. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission has promul-
gated a regulation describing what constitutes a "material 
financial effect", 
Basically, the regulation sets forth 
three alternative tests. 
Those tests are (1) the annual gross 
revenue test and (2) the annual net income test and (3) the 
current asset test. 
2 Cal.Admin.Code S18702(b). 
Under the Commission's r 
ulations, a governmental 
decision that will increase or decrease the annual gross 
revenues by $100,000 or more is "significant" and will have a 
"material financial effect". 
Turning now to the facts of this 
case, given today's utility bills one can safely assume that 
an average monthly utility bill for the 600 units proposed for 
the Frates Ranch development will be at least $40 per month. 
The annual utility bill per unit will thus be at least $480. 
P G & E's total annual revenues from the 600 proposed units 
would be $288,000. 
This exceeds the $100,000 gross revenue 
test promulgated by 
he Fair Political Practices Commission. 
Therefore, Commissioner 
tt must disqu lify himself and not 
participate in any way in LAFCO's d cision on 
hat annexation 
proposal. 
Given the facts outlined above, there is no need to 
discuss whether or not Commissioner Boyett also meets the 
capital asset test that would 
a 
Ii 
to th 
60-acre 
ific Gas 
Electric 
ny pr 
rty. 
Li ewise, there is 


Hack Boyett 
Page Four 
September 29, 1981 
no need to determine whether or not the annual net income test 
is met. 
I have reviewed the facts of this case and the ap-
plicable law with 
he staff at the Fair Political Practices 
Commission in Sacramento. 
The staff concurs with this opin-
ion.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a LAFCO commissioner who is a Pacific Gas & Electric Company manager and shareholder must disqualify himself from voting on annexation proposals based on the $100,000+ gross revenue conflict-of-interest threshold, and if not barred, what alternative thresholds ($200k, $300k, $1M) should apply.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr, old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter (not an FPPC response), so no CONCLUSION or CONCLUSION_SYNTHETIC applies. The document includes a September 29, 1981 letter from Deputy County Counsel Prentice Fish to Boyett that analyzes the conflict of interest issue, concluding Boyett must disqualify himself from the Frates Ranch matter. The primary February 9, 1982 letter requests FPPC guidance on the general $100,000+ gross revenue threshold and whether alternative thresholds ($200k, $300k, $1M) should apply. OCR artifacts and formatting issues present but content is substantially clear.; LLM: This is an incoming request letter (not an FPPC response), so no CONCLUSION or CONCLUSION_SYNTHETIC sections apply. Document includes embedded prior opinion letter from Deputy County Counsel Prentice Fish dated September 29, 1981, which does contain conclusions regarding the specific Frates Ranch matter. Primary question concerns general applicability of the $100,000 threshold and whether higher thresholds should apply. OCR artifacts present but content substantially clear. February 9, 1982 date appears correct despite 'February 9, 1932' typo in body text.

Re: 
H. E. Boyett; Conflict 
Local Agency Formation 
Dear Prentice: 
This letter is written to you on behalf of my long time 
friend, Mr. H. E. Boyett, who serves as manager for Pacific Gas 
& Electric Company in the Sonoma County area and also is an appointed 
member on the Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission. 
It is my understanding that you will shortly be submitting 
a request for an advisory opinion to the Executive Director of the 
California Fair Political Practice Conmission concerning the 
question of a possible conflict of interest on the part of Mr. 
Boyett when acting upon annexation projects such as the Petaluma 
annexation and the Frates residential/industrial project. 
It is my 
further understanding that the chief concern in connection with 
such matters is the possibility that the Frates project, or similar 
projects, if completed, would generate an annual gross revenue from 
the sale of natural gas and electricity to Pacific Gas & Electric 
Company in excess of the $100,000.00 amount suggested by the regu-
lations of the Fair Political Practice Commission. 
As an attorney who has served as political advisor for 
various local public agencies over the past twenty-five years I 
welcomed the enactment of the Political Reform Act of 1974 as a means 
of giving some definition to the continuing problem of conflict 
of interest. 
However, I am equally concerned with interpretations 
of that Act which place an unfair burden on the conscientous efforts 
of people like Mr. Boyett who donate their time and effort to 
public services. 
Accordingly I would request that consideration be given 
by you or the staff of the Fair Political Practice Commission to 
the following: 
'l'HE FINANCIAL INTEREST MUST BE SIGNIFICANT. 
A public 0 
he knows he had a 
If 
cial is not to act upon any matter in which 
al interest." 
(Gov. Code §87l00) 
An 


Mr. Prentice Fish 
Page 
Two 
January 13, 1982 
official has a financial interest when it is "reasonably foresee-
able that the decision will have a material financial effect" on 
either his ownership interest or management responsibilities in 
a "business entity." 
(See Gov. Code 7103 
The financial effect 
of the governmental decision or financial interest of a public 
official is material only if the decision will have a "significant 
effect" on the business entity, and in this regard some "considera-
tion" should be given to a circumstance where the annual gross 
revenue of the business entity would be increased by $100,000 or 
mo re . 
( Re g s. 18702 ) 
The regulation cited above indicates that any increase 
in gross annual revenues in excess of $100,000 is only a "consider-
ation" to be utilized in determining whether the project would 
have a "significant effect" on the financial interest of the 
public officer. 
It is my understanding that the estimated annual 
revenue of the Petaluma Annexation (Frates Project) is in excess 
of $100,000 and perhaps as high as $288,000 per year. 
When you 
consider that this figure constitutes only Ij200th of one percent 
of the gross annual revenue of PG&E for 1980 totalling five and 
one-quarter billion dollars ($5,250,000,000), and when this is 
accompanied by the fact that Mr. Boyett is only a local manager for 
PG&E it seems difficult to believe that any reasonably minded person 
would conclude that such would have a material effect upon a 
"financial interest" of Mr. Boyett. 
CONSIDERATION SHOULD BE GIVEN TO THE FACT THAT PG&E IS A 
PUBLIC ENTITY AND NOT STRICTLY A PRIVATE BUSINESS ENTITY. 
Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act and the regulations 
promulgated to assist in 
interpretation of that Act were drafted 
with private pro 
t making business entities in mind. 
(See Gov. 
Code 
82005 
In applying these rules and regulations it should be 
considered that a quasi 
1 
entity such as Pacific Gas & Electric 
Company cannot operate in the same manner as other large private 
businesses such as Exxon, Sears, etc. 
These large profit making 
companies when considering a project which is subject to the 


Mr. Prentice Fish 
Page Three 
January 13, 1982 
discretionary review of a local public agency, will presumably 
proceed with the project if such project can be carried out at a 
profit. 
PG&E is a quasi-public entity providing gas and electric 
service to all development projects which services are just as 
essential to the project as public sewer and water. 
PG&E, as a 
highly regulated governmental monopoly, has no discretion as to 
whether or not to provide these services to the project. 
Whether 
such services will be provided to a specific project at a profit 
depends not upon the project itself but on the large picture of 
the company's operation as a whole as determined by the State Public 
utilities Commission. 
This is not the type of beneficial 
"financial interest" which the framers of the Political Reform Act 
had in mind when drafting its provisions and promulgating its 
regulations. 
This is not to say that all public utilities are 
not within the purview of the Political Reform Act, as there may 
well be cases where the public utility itself is applying for a 
discretionary development permit which would be a benefit strictly 
to the public utility company itself. 
However where the public 
utility is simply performing the function required of it by the 
State of California in supplying gas and electric power to develop-
ment projects within the State, it would not seem to be receiving 
the beneficial "financial" effect anticipated by Chapter 7 of the 
Political Reform Act. 
ANY 1J'lATERIAL FINANCIAL EFFECT NUST BE "REASONABLY FORESEEABLE"; 
LAFCO HAS NO AUTHORITY TO APPROVE OR REJECT A DEVELOPMENT. 
A public officer has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act only if it is 
"reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect" upon him. 
(Gov. Code 
The Political Reform Act, and the regulations 
reunder, 
were dra 
d with the thought in mind that the public of 
cer is 
acting upon a discreti 
permit which, if 
d t would allow 
the applicant to proceed with a development which is of some 
benefit to the applicant. 
Such is not the case with a Local Agency 


Mr. Prentice Fish 
Page Four 
January 13, 1982 
Formation Commission which is specifically prohibited by statute 
from approving or disapproving or in any manner regulating any 
development project. 
(Gov. Code 
The only issue 
before LAFCO is as to whether or not the specific property 
should or should not be annexed to a City. 
It is only b~ the sub-
sequent action of the City, acting under its Subdivision and 
Zoning Ordinances, that it is determined whether some, if any, 
type of development will or will not be approved within the area 
annexed. 
This makes it extremely difficult for a member of LAFCO 
when called upon to act upon an annexation, to "reasonbly foresee" 
that he mayor may not have a material financial interest in 
some project which the City may see fit to approve within the 
annexed area. 
INTERPRETATIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFOm4 ACT. 
The problem of Mr. Boyett, as a member of LAFCO, in 
determining whether or not he is 
\vi th a conflict of interest 
under the Political Reform Act, is not one that can be considered 
in isolation. 
There are undoubtedly large numbers of other highly 
qualified employees of the major utility companies serving Calif-
ornia, i.e. PG&E, So. Cal. Edison, Mut. Water Dist, Sacto. M.D.D., 
Pac. Tel. & Tel, et aI, who donate the services to local government. 
Certainly it is sound public policy to encourage such service. 
Interpretations of the Political Reform Act should not be so strained 
as to seriously inte 
re with the tremendous public benefits 
obtained from those who donate their time and energy to public service. 
It is sincerely hoped that your office as well as the 
staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission will see fit to 
recognize the remoteness of the 
al interest of Mr.Boyett to 
the annexation projects, which come before him in his capacity as 
a member of LAFCO.
embedding.first_500_words: A-82-029 State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 327-5562 322-3660 322-5901 392-6441 322-6444 March 16, 1982 Prentice Fish Deputy County Counsel County Administration Center 2555 Mendocino Avenue Santa Rosa, CA 95401 Dear Mr. Fish: Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this office on behalf of Mr. Hack Boyett, a member of the Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission. Based on our conversation, your letter of February 9, 1982, and the other correspondence attached to your letter, my understanding of the situation is as follows. The Sonoma County Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO) is organized pursuant to Government Code Sections 54773, et seq. It approves or denies requests for the annexation of property to cities. The Sonoma County LAFCO has a written policy requiring the applicant-city to prezone the area proposed for annexation before it will act on the proposal. If the annexation is approved, further city permits will have to be acquired before actual development can proceed. In our phone conversation in October and your letter of September 29, 1981, the Frates Ranch Annexation proposal was the subject of discussion. This annexation proposal, which was subsequently withdrawn before LAFCO had voted on it, involved approximately 700 acres. It was proposed that approximately 200 acres would be used for a business park, 105 acres for approximately 600 residential units, and 232 acres for a golf course. Approximately 60 acres of Frates Ranch is owned by Pacific Gas and Electric Company which uses it presently as a substation. _____________________ 1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. Prentice Fish March 16, 1982 Page Two Hack Boyett, the public member of Sonoma County LAFCO, is the Sonoma County Regional Manager for the Pacific Gas and Electric Company. He also owns stock valued between $10,000 and $100,000 in the Pacific Gas and Electric Company. You concluded in your letter of September 29, 1981, and I concurred, that Mr. Boyett would be required to disqualify himself and not participate in any way in LAFCO's decision on the Frates Ranch annexation proposal since the foreseeable financial effect on PG&E's annual gross revenues far exceeded $100,000. This conclusion was based in part on Commission Regulation 18702(b)(A)(1) which reads in relevant part: (1) Whether, in the case of a business entity in which the public official holds a direct or indirect investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more or in the case of a business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, employee, trustee or holds any position of management, the effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease: (A) The annualized gross revenues by the lesser of: 1. One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or 2. One percent if it is one thousand dollars $1,000) or more. Even though the Frates
embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter dated February 9, 1982, from Sonoma County Counsel requesting FPPC advice on whether commissioner Hack Boyett's involvement with PG&E creates a disqualifying financial interest under the $100,000 gross revenue rule for LAFCO annexation decisions, with alternative threshold analysis. The letter incorporates a September 29, 1981 Deputy County Counsel opinion concluding Boyett must disqualify himself from the Frates Ranch matter, plus supporting correspondence.
